paragraphs.body: Easily Obtainable Self Destruction

annotations.comment: I like this title.

paragraphs.body: Mark Fisher is a lot of things: recruitment chair, player, king in his court. The party expands and contracts around him, in for a dab and then out of his way, leaving space for the next waiting hand and overflowing cup. An ocean of boys, and none of them matter, not when a real man like Fisher is in the room. In his midst, we are nothing.

annotations.comment: Love this beginning. I think this is a frat party, but help us know for sure.

paragraphs.body: “Dude!” he shouts, spotting me from across the room. He walks over, stepping over the twisted body of a fallen freshman without even a glance. He’s used to such navigation, of course he is. I am searched in the awesome fire of his gaze and found worthy, rewritten in gold with a casual “so fucking good to see you man” and a firm pat on the back. In his presence, I forget that I’m not very funny, that I’m freckled and kind of stretched looking in a way that Becky, my roommate’s hot girlfriend, once described as “slenderman-esque.”

annotations.comment: I like how you tightened the focus to this particular narrator. I did think you might include an “I” in the first paragraph, too.
I love this but felt that the decision to yell “Dude!” Showed his approval already.
annotations.context_left: d a firm pat on the

annotations.comment: I laughed out loud.

paragraphs.body: Mark sways slightly, beer sloshing over the lip of his cup and forming a sticky trail down his forearm. I can’t stand that kind of texture, the way my wet arm hairs clump together, but Fisher is unphased. There are names for people like Fisher, names like GOAT and badass, and there are names for people like me, names that don’t bear repeating. I didn’t even know that Fisher knew me. I’m not sure how he could. I tend to hang back at parties, talk to a couple of friends, and then get bored and leave before anything is really over.

annotations.comment: I’d call him Fisher throughout.
annotations.context_left: down his forearm. I can’t stand that kind of texture, the way my wet arm hairs clump together, but
annotations.context_right: is unphased. There are names for people like Fisher, names like GOAT and badass, and there are name

annotations.comment: I’d like to know a little bit more about why he thinks Fisher singles him out (if he does think that).

paragraphs.body: “This music,” says Mark, swallowing heavily, “is shit.” And he’s totally right because it totally is. It’s more of a series of sounds than any sort of melody. The backbeat pounds in my head, taking up space between my eyes and pulsing there.

I don’t really speak, but he must sense my agreement because he grabs my arm with an extended cry of “partyyy” and manhandles me to a table of shot glasses and bottles. There’s jagger, gin, vodka, a mostly empty tequila that strays pretty heavily from the Oktoberfest theme. Fisher takes eight shot glasses and pours the both of us one of each. I have never been as close to greatness as I am at this moment.

annotations.comment: Again, made me laugh
I got a little confused here — one of each… bottle?
I’m still wondering if he’s not more starstruck or something akin to that? His actions and dialogue seem pretty chill, but the narration is telling us this is a big deal.
annotations.context_left: ess as I am at this

paragraphs.body: He lines up the shots, looks me in the eye, and then they’re gone, swallowed away like they are just water and he’s dying of thirst. I eye my own shots distrustfully, I’m not much for hard liquor, but he tells me to just fucking do it man and to not be a pussy, and then I’ve put back all three, and I’m coughing. My eyes water, but he’s cheering, and when Fisher cheers the world cheers, and I may be no king of the party but in his midst, I am for the first time within the court.

annotations.text: the first time within the court.
annotations.comment: I think what’s bugging me here is that he never seemed to have to struggle to get to this point. He was in the court from the beginning.

paragraphs.body: Fisher introduces me to a girl whose name is Cindy. She’s pretty, really pretty, and I fumble as I tell her that, but she seems to find it charming.
“I like your costume,” she says. “Phineas, right? From the cartoon?”
I look down, confused, at the stripey orange shirt my mother bought me at Target.
“Exactly,” yells Fisher. “My boy Steve’s a man of culture.”

It becomes clear, in this moment, that Fisher thinks I’m someone else. I do consider telling him that my name isn’t Steve– it’s Garren, after my father’s second least favorite Uncle– but Steve is a good name. A better name even. Steve is the kind of guy that gets to be Fisher’s boy. He’s a part of something bigger than a nothing dork like Garren could ever dream.

annotations.comment: Ha! Very funny.

paragraphs.body: Steve is, apparently, wearing a costume at a completely costumeless party, but I shrug, real cool, and pretend it’s intentional. At her request I say “I know what we’re gonna do today” in my best impression of a show I’ve never seen. It’s totally working, and she’s totally feeling me.

annotations.comment: Yeah, I wanted to see this playing it cool guy earlier.

I might like a little context for how this guy ended up at the party and what he expected to have happen vs. what is actually happen. I assume he didn’t think Fisher was going to immediately be into him?
annotations.context_left: Steve is, apparently, wearing a costu
annotations.context_right: at a completely costumeless party, but I shrug, real cool, and pretend it’s intentional. At her req

paragraphs.body: Fisher hands us each a shot, downing three more himself as Cindy giggles. I hold the room temperature liquid in my sweaty palm, putting it down and to the side when they get distracted in an effortless conversation about something I can’t really follow. Fisher talks like he’s underwater, and he’s practically glowing, just like he always is, so I don’t care that I can’t tell what he’s saying. Not many men can have this much presence this many shots in.

annotations.comment: What does this main character want? The story is funny and charming but a little flat, since I don’t see what he’s trying to get.

paragraphs.body: The song cuts off mid line, harshly switching to something loud and Mr. Worldwide, and then another guy is calling for Fish Man and Fisher slips away from us and out the door, taking the easiness of the conversation with him.
There is a moment of silence. Cindy looks at me. I search the room for something to talk about. In the corner, the sound of girl retching is just loud enough to be heard over the music.
“So,” I ask Cindy, stilted. “What’s your major?”
In my mind I calculate how long it will take me to fumble this girl.

We talk for a little while longer. She’s undecided in her major, and from just outside of Philly, and she seems increasingly eager to get away from this conversation, but her friends ditched her for another party, and they forgot to tell her where they’re going. Apparently, they do that a lot. Apparently, her friend Jessica is, what she calls, a total bitch.

annotations.comment: Is Cindy texting during this conversation?Still not sure what the MC wants.

paragraphs.body: She asks if she can borrow a cigarette, and it goes against every fiber of my being to tell her I don’t smoke, thinking of the videos my mom likes to send about lung cancer and the fentanyl crisis.
“I don’t either,” she says. “Drunk cigarettes don’t count.”

annotations.comment: Oh, Cindy.

paragraphs.body: She walks up to some other guy, gigglingly placing her arm on his bicep. I look away, check my phone. I haven’t been here nearly long enough to leave without facing the laughter of my roommate.

annotations.comment: Is it common for people to go to parties alone? That feels so strange to me. But I am an old.

paragraphs.body: I am deciding if the light mockery is worth withstanding when, there’s a tap on my shoulder, and there’s Cindy. She holds her closed fist out to me, and says “guess what I found,” before opening to reveal two cigarettes. She takes me by the arm and pulls me up and through the door, and I laugh a little uncomfortably as I follow, but I know better than to let an opportunity like this slide.

annotations.comment: I’m always fascinated by characters who want the approval of people they don’t like.

paragraphs.body: I’m through the door, and then outside, and there’s a cigarette in her palm that I don’t want to smoke. There is a beautiful girl in front of me, and all I’m thinking about is charred, blackened lungs. I wonder if I’ll end up coughing up tar. I see my life stretched out in front of me, cut short by lung cancer at 38.

annotations.comment: I love this overthinker and would like to meet him earlier.

paragraphs.body: “I’ve always liked dorky guys,” she whispers like a secret, holding my clammy hand in her pretty one as she passes me a mistake I know I’ll make for her.

annotations.comment: Hilarious. (Although, sorry, again, what does he want?)

paragraphs.body: Her lighter is a piece of shit, and it takes three tries for her to get the flame steady. It’s when it finally catches that we hear him. Fisher, singing, a stretched and wobbly Pledge of Allegiance. We turn and there he is, on hands and knees on the rough pavement of the quad. We watch as Fisher crawls forward, shredding his own knees on the course ground. Behind him, there is a trail of blood that extends in a circle around the whole quad. As we watch, he pauses to hack up a wet splatter of bile, before continuing, crawling right over the vibrant, orangish liquid. Dotted around the circle, we can see more such splotches, can watch as a salsa-like chunkiness is slowly replaced by just an outpouring of liquid, remnants of a body turning itself inside out.

annotations.comment: This is an amazing and very gross image.

paragraphs.body: It's almost mythical. One must imagine heroes of old. Bruce Willis battered and broken and bleeding, glass wounds and gashes in both feet, and yet unwilling, unable to surrender. Mel Gibson, despite all his years of military training, tortured and dripping, but only more heroic for his pain. It’s glorious.

annotations.text: It's almost mythical. One must imagine heroes of old
annotations.comment: I sort of wanted him to share this observation with Cindy.

paragraphs.body: Cindy, unable to understand the beauty before her, drops both lighter and cigarette to run to Fisher. I follow.
She steps in front of Fisher, earning a noise of frustration when she blocks his path. The singing goes quiet. She leans down to him.
“Are you alright?” she demands. Fisher replies in only sounds.
“He’s fine,” I tell her. “He’s just a little drunk. He’s fine.”
“Are you fucking stupid?” She whirls around. “This is your fucking friend. And he’s bleeding.”
“Yeah,” I admit. “But in, like, a cool way.”

annotations.comment: This made me laugh very hard.

paragraphs.body: She just looks at me.
“Like Iron Man.”
She rolls her eyes, big and annoyed, and turns back to Fisher with a “hey Mark, I’m gonna make sure you’re okay, alright?”

“He’s fine, Cindy. Don’t be a buzzkill. He’s Fisher.” She isn’t listening to me. I pull on her arm, growing desperate. “He’s always going to be fine. He’s a fucking god, Cindy. Come smoke with me.” I’m pulling her arm, dragging her up and away, and she turns, and she back hands me, every finger and ring connecting with the side of my face.

annotations.comment: Here, I sort of didn’t buy that the MC would be that aggressive to her.
Nice.

paragraphs.body: It hurts.
“Get the fuck away from me,” she says.
And with that I’m dismissed. Unimportant. Ignored. Relegated back to a nothing, away from the glory and the action.

In the light of the porch, the world seems far away. From the ground, I pick up one of the cigarettes, craving some easily obtainable self-destruction, but, finding myself obviously lighterless, I just put it in my pocket. I turn, walking back into the party.

annotations.comment: I wanted a little more from this moment — in fact, wanted this scene to be a little longer. Perhaps more of him trying to convince Cindy Fisher is fine, and/or more of a moment of realizing that Fisher is not fine? Joseph, another strong story. You’re a good writer! I had a lot of fun watching this unfold and just enjoying (from my elderly POV) how young everyone here seems. I do think we need more context — why did not-Steve go to this party, and how does he already know Fisher, and why is he buy himself? And then think about what not-Steve wants over the course of this party? At first, it felt like he wanted Fisher’s attention, but he got that easily, and then it seemed like he wanted to get with Cindy but that also seemed pretty easy. So I’m left wondering a bit what it is he wants, and how the final sequence, with Fisher falling apart, Cindy trying to help and the MC just not being helpful at all — resolves this conflict and plot. Happy to talk it over if you like! Terrific draft. Can’t wait to see what else you write!
